children.value:
The differentiation rules assembled in chapter 6 give us a systematic way to
compute the derivative

children.html: f′f'f′

children.value:
of any combination of elementary functions. That
derivative is itself a function, defined on the same domain, and there is
nothing preventing us from applying the same rules to it again. Repeating the
process produces the higher-order derivatives, each one encoding a new layer
of information about the original function: not just how fast

children.html: fff

children.value:
is changing,
but how that rate of change is itself evolving, and then how that evolution is
evolving in turn.
You know the definition of the

children.html: f′′(x)=(f′(x))′f''(x) = \bigl(f'(x)\bigr)'f′′(x)=(f′(x))′
nnn
f(n)(x)f^{(n)}(x)f(n)(x)

children.value: You are familiar with the notations

children.html: f′′(x)f''(x)f′′(x)
d2fdx2\dfrac{d^2f}{dx^2}dx2d2f​
f(n)(x)f^{(n)}(x)f(n)(x)

children.value: You can compute higher-order derivatives of polynomials, exponential functions, and trigonometric functions.
What happens when we differentiate the derivative?

children.label: What happens when we differentiate the derivative?

children.value: In chapter 5 we derived the derivative function

children.html: f′(x)=0.04x−0.6f'(x) = 0.04x - 0.6f′(x)=0.04x−0.6
f(x)=0.02x2−0.6x+6f(x) = 0.02x^2 - 0.6x + 6f(x)=0.02x2−0.6x+6
(f′(x))′=(0.04x−0.6)′=0.04.\bigl(f'(x)\bigr)' = (0.04x - 0.6)' = 0.04.(f′(x))′=(0.04x−0.6)′=0.04.

children.value:
The result is a positive constant. This constant positive value means that the
slope of the marble track increases at the same rate everywhere along the track:
for each additional centimeter of horizontal distance the slope rises by exactly

. The track curves upward at a constant rate along its entire length,
which is exactly what we expect from a parabola.
This new function is called the

children.html: fff
f′′(x)f''(x)f′′(x)

children.value:
. It is the derivative of the derivative, obtained by applying the
differentiation process twice.

children.alt:
Graph of the marble track height profile together with its first and second
derivatives, showing the curve, the tangent slope, and the constant slope change
at a common position x = 10. (Source: own figure; licence CC BY-SA
4.0)

children.value:
Graph of the marble track height profile together with its first and second
derivatives, showing the curve, the tangent slope, and the constant slope change
at a common position

children.html: x=10x = 10x=10

children.value:
We do not have to stop there. The second derivative is itself a function, so we
can differentiate it again. Differentiating a third time gives the

children.html: f′′′(x)f'''(x)f′′′(x)
f(4)(x)f^{(4)}(x)f(4)(x)

children.value:
, and so on. For the
marble track the third derivative is

children.html: f′′′(x)=(0.04)′=0f'''(x) = (0.04)' = 0f′′′(x)=(0.04)′=0

children.value:
, because the second
derivative is constant and the derivative of a constant is zero. For this
quadratic function, every derivative of order three or higher is therefore equal
to zero.

children.html: nnn

children.value:
Applying the differentiation rules repeatedly produces a sequence of
functions. Writing

children.html: f(0)=ff^{(0)} = ff(0)=f
f(1)=f′f^{(1)} = f'f(1)=f′

children.value:
for the first derivative, each subsequent member of the sequence is defined by
one further differentiation:

children.html: f(n)(x)=(f(n−1)(x))′.f^{(n)}(x) = \Bigl(f^{(n-1)}(x)\Bigr)'.f(n)(x)=(f(n−1)(x))′.

f′=f(1)f' = f^{(1)}f′=f(1)
f′′=f(2)f'' = f^{(2)}f′′=f(2)

The Leibniz notation for higher derivatives extends the first-derivative
symbol

children.html: dfdx\dfrac{df}{dx}dxdf​

children.value:
f''(x) = \frac{d^2 f}{dx^2}, \qquad
f'''(x) = \frac{d^3 f}{dx^3}, \qquad
f^{(n)}(x) = \frac{d^n f}{dx^n}.
children.html: f′′(x)=d2fdx2,f′′′(x)=d3fdx3,f(n)(x)=dnfdxn.f''(x) = \frac{d^2 f}{dx^2}, \qquad f'''(x) = \frac{d^3 f}{dx^3}, \qquad f^{(n)}(x) = \frac{d^n f}{dx^n}.f′′(x)=dx2d2f​,f′′′(x)=dx3d3f​,f(n)(x)=dxndnf​.
ddd

children.value:
counts how many times we differentiate, and the
matching superscript on

children.html: dxdxdx

children.value:
keeps the dimensional bookkeeping consistent.
Both families of notation are standard in engineering literature.
How do we compute the higher derivatives for a roller coaster lift hill?

children.label: How do we compute the higher derivatives for a roller coaster lift hill?

children.value:
We now introduce the running example for this chapter, a section of a roller
coaster lift hill. The track carries passengers from the boarding platform at
ground level up to the summit of the first drop. We place the origin at the
base of the lift and measure height

children.html: hhh
xxx

children.value:
both in
meters. The shape of the track is modeled by the cubic polynomial

children.html: h(x)=130 x2−12700 x3,x∈[0,60].h(x) = \frac{1}{30}\,x^2 - \frac{1}{2700}\,x^3, \quad x \in [0, 60].h(x)=301​x2−27001​x3,x∈[0,60].

children.alt:
Graph of the roller coaster lift hill height profile h(x) = \dfrac{1}{30}x^2 -
\dfrac{1}{2700}x^3 on the interval [0, 60], showing the base at (0, 0) and
the summit at (60, 40). (Source: own figure; licence CC BY-SA
4.0)

children.value: Graph of the roller coaster lift hill height profile

(60,40)(60, 40)(60,40)

Two boundary conditions are built into this formula. At the boarding
platform we have

children.html: h(0)=0h(0) = 0h(0)=0

, so the track starts at ground level. At the
summit,

40 m40~\text{m}40 m

x=60x = 60x=60

children.value:
We now apply the power rule and constant multiple rule from chapter 6 to
compute the successive derivatives. The first derivative gives the gradient,
that is, the slope of the track at each horizontal position:

children.html: h′(x)=x15−x2900=x(60−x)900.h'(x) = \frac{x}{15} - \frac{x^2}{900} = \frac{x(60 - x)}{900}.h′(x)=15x​−900x2​=900x(60−x)​.

interval [0, 60], showing the horizontal start and end of the lift hill and
the maximum slope at x = 30~\text{m}. (Source: own figure; licence CC BY-SA
4.0)

[0,60][0, 60][0,60]

, showing the horizontal start and end of the lift hill and
the maximum slope at

(60−x)(60 - x)(60−x)
h′(0)=0h'(0) = 0h′(0)=0

, so the track
starts horizontally at the boarding platform and levels off again at the

the track, which we will examine carefully in sections 7.3 and 7.4.

children.html: h(4)(x)=0h^{(4)}(x) = 0h(4)(x)=0

children.value:
, and all higher derivatives
are zero. This is exactly what we expect. The height profile

children.html: hhh

children.value:
is a
polynomial of degree three, and differentiating three times reduces it to a
nonzero constant. Every derivative of order four or higher is therefore
identically zero.
How do higher-order derivatives behave for other function families?

children.label: How do higher-order derivatives behave for other function families?

children.value:
For polynomials the pattern is systematic. Each differentiation reduces the
degree by one, and after

children.html: nnn
nnn

children.value:
polynomial we
reach a nonzero constant. One step further gives zero.

children.html: exe^xex

children.value:
behaves very differently. Its derivative is
itself:

children.html: (ex)′=ex,(ex)′′=ex,…,(ex)(n)=ex.(e^x)' = e^x, \quad (e^x)'' = e^x, \quad \ldots, \quad (e^x)^{(n)} = e^x.(ex)′=ex,(ex)′′=ex,…,(ex)(n)=ex.

children.value: No matter how many times we differentiate,

children.html: exe^xex

children.value:
remains unchanged. This
remarkable stability is unique to the exponential and lies at the heart of why

children.html: exe^xex

children.value:
solves a wide class of differential equations, which will become central
in later courses on dynamics and systems theory.
form a four-step cycle. Starting from

children.html: sin⁡x\sin xsinx

children.value:
(\sin x)' = \cos x, \quad (\cos x)' = -\sin x, \quad (-\sin x)' = -\cos x,
\quad (-\cos x)' = \sin x.
children.html: (sin⁡x)′=cos⁡x,(cos⁡x)′=−sin⁡x,(−sin⁡x)′=−cos⁡x,(−cos⁡x)′=sin⁡x.(\sin x)' = \cos x, \quad (\cos x)' = -\sin x, \quad (-\sin x)' = -\cos x, \quad (-\cos x)' = \sin x.(sinx)′=cosx,(cosx)′=−sinx,(−sinx)′=−cosx,(−cosx)′=sinx.

children.value: After four differentiations we return to

children.html: sin⁡x\sin xsinx
(sin⁡x)(4)=sin⁡x(\sin x)^{(4)} = \sin x(sinx)(4)=sinx

children.value:
.
The same four-step cycle holds for

children.html: cos⁡x\cos xcosx
nnn

children.value:
-th derivative by repeated application of the
differentiation rules of chapter 6, and we have established the standard
notations. For the roller coaster height profile

children.html: h(x)=130x2−12700x3h(x) = \frac{1}{30}x^2 - \frac{1}{2700}x^3h(x)=301​x2−27001​x3

children.value:
, the first derivative describes
the track gradient, the second derivative describes how quickly that gradient
is changing, and the third derivative is a negative constant that reflects the
cubic structure of the polynomial. The exponential function and the
trigonometric functions show that higher derivatives need not simplify or
vanish: they can remain constant or cycle indefinitely.

In the next section we turn to the direct physical meaning of these successive
derivatives when the independent variable is time. A position function, its
first derivative, and its second derivative tell the story of position,
velocity, and acceleration. The third derivative has a name of its own, and an
engineering significance that every designer of moving systems needs to know.
